subject: family_law
fact_pattern: Sandra and her husband Leon have been married for eight years. Leon is the sole registered owner of the family home in Toronto, where the couple lives and has lived throughout the marriage. They also own a cottage in Muskoka (registered jointly) where they spend four to six weeks each summer. Leon argues that the Muskoka cottage — not the Toronto home — is the matrimonial home because they spend their most enjoyable time there and he considers it their 'real' home. He further argues that Sandra has no possession rights because her name does not appear on the Toronto home's title.
call_of_question: Under the Family Law Act, which of the following most accurately describes Sandra's rights with respect to the Toronto home?

options.A: Sandra has no right to possession of the Toronto home because she is not a registered owner and has never been on title.
options.B: The Toronto home qualifies as a matrimonial home because it is ordinarily occupied by the spouses as their family residence; Sandra has an equal right to possession under FLA s. 19(1) regardless of which spouse holds title.
options.C: Sandra has no right to possession of the Toronto home because the Muskoka cottage, jointly owned by both spouses, is the matrimonial home — only one property can be designated a matrimonial home, and the cottage qualifies because both spouses prefer it.
options.D: Sandra's right to possession of the Toronto home depends on her having contributed to mortgage payments or household expenses during the marriage.

explanation: Under FLA s. 18(1), a 'matrimonial home' is every property in which a person has an interest and that is or, if the spouses have separated, was at the time of separation ordinarily occupied by the person and his or her spouse as their family residence. The Toronto home, occupied throughout the marriage, clearly qualifies. Importantly, under FLA s. 18, there can be more than one matrimonial home — both the Toronto home and the Muskoka cottage could qualify if both are ordinarily occupied as a family residence. Leon cannot exclude the Toronto home from matrimonial home status simply by asserting a preference for the cottage. Once the Toronto home is a matrimonial home, FLA s. 19(1) grants both spouses an equal right to possession — regardless of which spouse owns it or is on title.
why_A_wrong: A reflects the Ownership-Determines-Possession Trap. FLA s. 19(1) expressly grants both spouses an equal right to possession of every matrimonial home regardless of which spouse owns it or appears on title. Ownership and possession rights under the FLA are entirely distinct. Legal title in one spouse does not deprive the other spouse of the s. 19(1) equal right to possession.
why_B_wrong: B is correct. The Toronto home is a matrimonial home under FLA s. 18 because the spouses ordinarily occupy it as their family residence. FLA s. 19(1) grants Sandra an equal right to possession regardless of ownership. Leon's argument that the cottage is the 'real' home is irrelevant — a couple can have more than one matrimonial home, and the Toronto home's ordinary occupancy throughout the marriage qualifies it independently.
why_C_wrong: C applies two errors. The Multiple-Residence Trap: the FLA does not limit spouses to one matrimonial home. Both the Toronto home and the Muskoka cottage could qualify as matrimonial homes simultaneously if both are ordinarily occupied as family residences. The Primary-Residence-Required Trap: a property need not be the primary or preferred residence to qualify as a matrimonial home — ordinary occupancy as a family residence suffices. Even if the cottage is also a matrimonial home, this does not displace the Toronto home's status.
why_D_wrong: D invents a financial-contribution requirement. The Financial-Contribution Trap: FLA s. 19(1) grants the right to possession based on marriage and the property being a matrimonial home — not on whether the non-titled spouse contributed to mortgage payments or household expenses. The right arises by statute regardless of each spouse's financial contribution to the property.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Equal Right of Possession — s. 19
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 19(1)

tested_concepts: FLA s.18 matrimonial home definition — ordinary occupation
multiple properties can qualify as matrimonial homes simultaneously
FLA s.19(1) equal possession regardless of title

subject: family_law
fact_pattern: After separation, the husband moves out of the matrimonial home but continues to pay the mortgage. He later argues he is entitled to exclusive possession of the home because he is the sole owner and the sole financial supporter of the property.
call_of_question: Is the husband automatically entitled to exclusive possession of the matrimonial home because he is the owner and pays the mortgage?

options.A: Yes — as owner and financial supporter, the husband is automatically entitled to exclusive possession.
options.B: Yes — once a spouse voluntarily leaves the matrimonial home, the remaining spouse automatically has exclusive possession.
options.C: No — both spouses retain their equal right of possession under s.19(1) regardless of ownership; a spouse seeking exclusive possession must obtain a court order under FLA s.24, which the court grants only where it considers it appropriate having regard to the best interests of any child or the personal protection of a spouse.
options.D: Yes — once one spouse has moved out, the remaining spouse obtains exclusive possession by default.

explanation: Ownership and financial contribution do not automatically confer exclusive possession under the FLA. FLA s.19(1) preserves equal possession rights for both spouses. To obtain exclusive possession, the husband would need a court order under s.24, where the court weighs statutory factors including the best interests of any children, financial position of each spouse, availability of other accommodation, and any violence.
why_A_wrong: Ownership alone does not give exclusive possession under the FLA. The s.19 right exists independently of title, and s.24 is the mechanism for exclusive possession orders.
why_B_wrong: A spouse who moves out does not lose their s.19 right of possession by doing so. Voluntary departure is not a waiver of possession rights.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Equal possession under s.19 persists; exclusive possession requires a s.24 court order.
why_D_wrong: No default rule grants exclusive possession to the remaining spouse. Both spouses retain s.19 rights until a court order is obtained or a domestic contract addresses the matter.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Equal Right of Possession — s. 19
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 19(1) and s. 24

subject: family_law
fact_pattern: A court is hearing a motion for an exclusive possession order in favour of the wife and the couple's two young children while the divorce proceedings are pending. The husband opposes, arguing the order cannot be made because he is the sole owner of the home.
call_of_question: What test does FLA s.24 apply when determining whether to grant an exclusive possession order?

options.A: Ownership of the home is the primary factor in awarding exclusive possession.
options.B: The court must grant exclusive possession if one spouse has left the home.
options.C: Under FLA s.24, the court considers: the best interests of the children, each spouse's financial position, the availability of other appropriate accommodation, any violence or abuse, and other relevant circumstances; ownership of the property is not determinative.
options.D: Exclusive possession can only be granted as part of a final divorce order.

explanation: FLA s.24(3) sets out the factors the court must consider on a motion for exclusive possession: the best interests of the children, the financial position of both spouses, availability of other appropriate accommodation, any violence or abuse, and any other relevant circumstances. Ownership is not determinative; a non-owning spouse may receive an exclusive possession order where the circumstances warrant it.
why_A_wrong: Ownership is expressly not determinative under s.24. The entire purpose of s.19 and s.24 is to separate possession rights from ownership rights.
why_B_wrong: A spouse leaving the home is not the test for exclusive possession. Departure may be relevant to the facts but is not a triggering condition under s.24.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.24(3) lists the multi-factor test; ownership is not determinative.
why_D_wrong: FLA s.24 expressly permits exclusive possession orders on an interim or temporary basis during proceedings, not only at the final order stage.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Exclusive Possession — s. 24 Test
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 24(3)

subject: family_law
fact_pattern: The husband moved out of the matrimonial home following a dispute. While he was away, the wife changed the locks and refused to give him a key, preventing him from returning. The husband now wishes to re-enter the home.
call_of_question: What right does the husband have with respect to possession of the matrimonial home after voluntarily leaving?

options.A: The wife may validly exclude the husband once she has changed the locks and physically asserted possession.
options.B: The husband has no right to re-enter the home once he has voluntarily left.
options.C: The husband retains his equal right to possession under FLA s.19 even after leaving; the wife cannot unilaterally exclude him; the husband may bring a motion to enforce his s.19 right, and the wife would need a court order under s.24 to validly bar his re-entry.
options.D: The husband must wait until the divorce proceedings are concluded before asserting his possession rights.

explanation: FLA s.19(1) grants both spouses an equal right to possession throughout the marriage. Voluntarily leaving the matrimonial home does not waive or extinguish this right. The wife cannot unilaterally exclude the husband by changing the locks; she would need to obtain a court order for exclusive possession under s.24. The husband may bring a motion to enforce his s.19 rights.
why_A_wrong: Changing the locks and asserting physical possession do not legally extinguish the husband's s.19 right. Self-help is not a recognized method of defeating possession rights under the FLA.
why_B_wrong: Voluntary departure from the matrimonial home does not constitute a waiver of s.19 rights. The right persists regardless of which spouse is currently residing in the home.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. s.19 rights persist after voluntary departure; exclusion requires a s.24 court order.
why_D_wrong: s.19 rights can be enforced at any time during the marriage and separation. There is no requirement to wait for the divorce to be concluded.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Equal Right of Possession — s. 19
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 19(1) and s. 24

tested_concepts: s.19 right persists after voluntary departure

subject: family_law
fact_pattern: A couple owns both a primary residence in the city and a cottage at which they spend summers. They ordinarily occupy both properties as their family residence throughout the year. Both properties are sold after separation. The wife challenges whether FLA s.19 applied to both.
call_of_question: Can both the city home and the cottage qualify as matrimonial homes to which FLA s.19 applies?

options.A: Only the primary residence is a matrimonial home; a cottage cannot qualify.
options.B: A property used only seasonally cannot be a matrimonial home.
options.C: Both properties can qualify as matrimonial homes under FLA s.18(2) if both were ordinarily occupied by both spouses as their family residence; FLA s.19 rights apply to each qualifying property.
options.D: The couple must have formally designated one property as the matrimonial home for s.19 to apply.

explanation: FLA s.18(1) defines a matrimonial home as every property in which a person has an interest and that is or was at the time of separation ordinarily occupied by the person and his or her spouse as their family residence. There is no limit to one matrimonial home per couple (s.18(2) permits multiple). Seasonal or summer use can satisfy 'ordinarily occupied' if the family regularly used the property as a family residence. Both properties can be matrimonial homes and s.19 applies to each.
why_A_wrong: The FLA does not restrict the matrimonial home definition to a single primary residence. Multiple properties may qualify under s.18(2).
why_B_wrong: Seasonal use can satisfy the 'ordinarily occupied' test if the family regularly used the property as their family residence during that season. The FLA does not require year-round occupation.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Multiple matrimonial homes are permitted; s.19 applies to each qualifying property.
why_D_wrong: Formal designation is not required for a property to be a matrimonial home. The test is ordinary occupation as a family residence. Designation (under s.20) has other effects but is not a prerequisite for s.19.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Multiple Matrimonial Homes — s. 18(2)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 18(1), s. 18(2), and s. 19

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Consent Required to Dispose of Matrimonial Home — s. 21
fact_pattern: The husband is the sole registered owner of the matrimonial home. Without telling his wife, he signs an agreement of purchase and sale to sell the home to a buyer. The wife has not consented to the sale.
call_of_question: What does FLA s.21(1) require before the husband may validly sell the matrimonial home?

options.A: The husband may sell his own property without restriction since he is the sole registered owner.
options.B: The husband must obtain the wife's written consent or joinder in the instrument, a court order authorizing the sale, or a prior release of the wife's Part II rights; failing any of these, the sale may be set aside.
options.C: The husband must notify the wife of the sale; her consent is not required.
options.D: The s.21(1) restriction applies only if the wife's name appears on title.

explanation: FLA s.21(1) provides that a spouse shall not dispose of or encumber an interest in the matrimonial home unless the other spouse joins in the instrument or consents in writing, or has released all rights under Part II by separation agreement, or a court order authorizes the disposition. Sole ownership of the matrimonial home does not exempt a spouse from this requirement.
why_A_wrong: Sole ownership does not exempt the husband from s.21(1). The restriction applies regardless of which spouse holds legal title to the matrimonial home.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Written consent/joinder, a court order, or a prior release are the three permitted paths under s.21(1).
why_C_wrong: Notification is not sufficient. FLA s.21(1) requires the non-owning spouse to actively join in the instrument or provide written consent, not merely be informed.
why_D_wrong: FLA s.21(1) applies regardless of whether the non-owning spouse is on title. The restriction protects the spouse's Part II rights, not their ownership interest.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Consent Required to Dispose — s. 21(1)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 21(1)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Consent Required to Dispose of Matrimonial Home — s. 21
fact_pattern: The wife signed a separation agreement that expressly released all her rights under Part II of the FLA with respect to the matrimonial home. The husband later sold the home to a third party without obtaining any further consent from the wife. The wife now argues the sale should be set aside because she never consented to the actual sale.
call_of_question: Can the wife succeed in having the sale set aside?

options.A: Yes — a separation agreement cannot override the requirements of s.21(1).
options.B: No — FLA s.21(1)(b) provides that no further consent is required where the non-owning spouse has released all Part II rights through a separation agreement.
options.C: Yes — the release in the separation agreement is only valid if the wife also received independent legal advice at the time she signed.
options.D: No — but only if the buyer was aware of the separation agreement.

explanation: FLA s.21(1)(b) expressly provides that a spouse may dispose of the matrimonial home without the other's further consent if the other spouse 'has released all rights under this Part by a separation agreement.' The wife's release in the separation agreement satisfies this statutory exception; she cannot now seek to set aside the sale.
why_A_wrong: A separation agreement that releases Part II rights is expressly recognized in s.21(1)(b) as a valid basis for proceeding without further consent. The agreement does override the general consent requirement when it covers a full Part II release.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. s.21(1)(b) makes a full Part II release in a separation agreement a complete answer to the consent requirement.
why_C_wrong: While independent legal advice is good practice and may affect the enforceability of the agreement on other grounds, it is not a prerequisite for the s.21(1)(b) exception to apply.
why_D_wrong: The buyer's awareness of the separation agreement is irrelevant to whether the s.21(1)(b) exception applies. The exception operates by virtue of the release itself.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Release by Separation Agreement — s. 21(1)(b)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 21(1)(b)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Consent Required to Dispose of Matrimonial Home — s. 21
fact_pattern: The husband sold the matrimonial home to a purchaser who had no knowledge that the property was the couple's matrimonial home. The wife had not consented to the sale. The wife seeks to have the transaction set aside under FLA s.21(3).
call_of_question: What effect does the purchaser's lack of notice of the matrimonial home status have on the wife's s.21(3) application?

options.A: The sale can always be set aside where the non-consenting spouse did not consent, regardless of what the purchaser knew.
options.B: The purchaser is protected; under FLA s.21(3), where a spouse disposes of the matrimonial home without the other's consent and the purchaser acquired for value without notice that the property was a matrimonial home, the court will not set aside the transaction.
options.C: The wife can set aside the sale regardless of what the purchaser knew, because s.21 consent is a prerequisite to a valid disposition.
options.D: The purchaser must demonstrate that they conducted a registry search before the court will protect the transaction.

explanation: FLA s.21(3) provides that where a spouse purports to dispose of the matrimonial home without the required consent, the court may set aside the transaction unless the purchaser acquired the interest for value without notice that the property was a matrimonial home at the time of the disposition. The bona fide purchaser for value without notice is protected, and the wife's application will fail.
why_A_wrong: s.21(3) expressly carves out a bona fide purchaser protection. The court's power to set aside the transaction is qualified by the purchaser's notice of the matrimonial home status.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.21(3) protects the purchaser for value without notice of the matrimonial home status.
why_C_wrong: This overstates the remedy. s.21(3) limits the set-aside remedy specifically to cases where the purchaser had notice. Without notice, the purchaser is protected.
why_D_wrong: FLA s.21(3) uses actual notice as the test, not constructive notice through a registry search obligation. The purchaser does not need to prove a registry search was conducted.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Purchaser for Value Without Notice — s. 21(3)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 21(3)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Consent Required to Dispose of Matrimonial Home — s. 21
fact_pattern: Husband and wife separate. The wife vacates the matrimonial home. The husband, as sole owner, assumes that now that they are separated and the wife has moved out, he may sell the home without her consent. He proceeds to list and sell the home without obtaining the wife's consent.
call_of_question: Does separation and the wife's departure from the matrimonial home extinguish her s.21 consent rights?

options.A: Yes — once spouses separate, the non-owning spouse loses all s.21 rights.
options.B: Yes — once the wife has vacated the home, she has abandoned her Part II rights.
options.C: No — FLA s.21 continues to apply after separation; the wife's consent is still required unless she has released her rights through a domestic contract, a court order has been obtained, or she has joined in the instrument.
options.D: Yes — separation extinguishes s.21 rights, but the husband must still obtain a court order confirming this.

explanation: FLA s.21(1) does not contain a separation exception. The consent requirement continues to apply after separation until one of the three exceptions is met: (a) the other spouse joins in the instrument or consents in writing, (b) the other spouse has released all Part II rights through a separation agreement or other domestic contract, or (c) a court order authorizes the disposition. Physical departure from the home does not constitute an abandonment of Part II rights.
why_A_wrong: Separation alone does not extinguish s.21 rights. The consent requirement continues post-separation until one of the statutory exceptions is satisfied.
why_B_wrong: Vacating the matrimonial home does not constitute a release or abandonment of Part II rights under the FLA. A formal release in a domestic contract or a court order is required.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. s.21 applies post-separation; departure does not extinguish consent rights.
why_D_wrong: Option D is internally inconsistent. If separation extinguished s.21 rights, no court order would be needed to confirm it. The premise is also wrong: separation alone does not extinguish s.21 rights.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Consent Required to Dispose — s. 21(1)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 21(1)

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Consent Required to Dispose of Matrimonial Home — s. 21
fact_pattern: During the marriage, the wife provided written consent to the sale of the matrimonial home. The agreement of purchase and sale was signed by the husband and the buyer in reliance on the wife's consent. Six months later, before the closing date, the wife purports to revoke her consent.
call_of_question: Can the wife effectively revoke her consent to the sale of the matrimonial home after the agreement of purchase and sale has been entered into in reliance on it?

options.A: Yes — consent can always be revoked before the transaction closes.
options.B: No — consent given under s.21 can never be revoked under any circumstances.
options.C: No — once a spouse has consented and the other party has relied on that consent by entering into a binding agreement of purchase and sale, the consent generally cannot be revoked; revocation may only be possible before any reliance occurs.
options.D: Yes — the wife may revoke consent at any time before the transfer is registered on title.

explanation: Once a binding agreement of purchase and sale has been entered into in reliance on the consenting spouse's consent, revocation is generally not effective to defeat the transaction. The consent was given to enable the very transaction that is now binding on the parties. Revocation before any party has relied on the consent might be possible, but once the transaction is made, the consent cannot be withdrawn to frustrate the sale. The purchaser's rights are also engaged.
why_A_wrong: Revocation is not available as of right at any time before closing. Once the agreement of purchase and sale is signed in reliance on the consent, revocation would be ineffective to defeat the binding transaction.
why_B_wrong: This overstates the rule. Revocation before any reliance might in principle be possible. The key constraint is that revocation after reliance is not effective.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Post-reliance revocation is generally not effective; the consent enabled the binding transaction.
why_D_wrong: Registration on title is not the relevant cut-off. The binding agreement of purchase and sale (signed before registration) creates obligations in reliance on the consent, and revocation at any point after that is generally not effective.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Revocation of Consent — s. 21
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 21(1)

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Special Rules for the Matrimonial Home
fact_pattern: Sylvie and her husband Bernard are separating. The matrimonial home is registered in Bernard's name only. Sylvie has two children, ages 7 and 10, who are enrolled in schools one block from the matrimonial home. Bernard has not committed any act of violence and has alternative accommodation available at his parents' home. Sylvie applies for exclusive possession of the matrimonial home on an interim motion.
call_of_question: Which of the following most accurately describes how a court should assess Sylvie's application for exclusive possession under FLA s. 24?

options.A: Sylvie's application must be dismissed because Bernard has not committed any act of violence; the absence of violence is a precondition to bringing an exclusive possession application.
options.B: The court must weigh the s. 24(3) factors including the best interests of the children (school proximity and disruption), the availability of alternative accommodation for Bernard, and any other relevant circumstances; ownership by Bernard is not a bar to the order and violence is not a precondition.
options.C: Because Bernard is the registered owner and has not committed violence, the court must give significant weight to his ownership interest — exclusive possession orders must be limited to situations involving family violence or immediate safety risk.
options.D: Exclusive possession can only be granted as part of a final parenting order; it is not available on an interim motion where final custody has not yet been determined.

explanation: Under FLA s. 24(1), the court may order exclusive possession of the matrimonial home or any part of it to one spouse, regardless of which spouse owns it, for a period of time. Section 24(3) requires the court to consider: (a) the best interests of the children; (b) the financial position of the spouses; (c) any written agreement; (d) the availability of other suitable and affordable accommodation; (e) any order or right to possession of the home; and (f) any violence committed by a spouse against the other or the children. Violence is one factor — not a precondition. Here, the children's school proximity and the disruption to their routine are strong factors under s. 24(3)(a), and Bernard's availability of alternative accommodation weighs in Sylvie's favour under s. 24(3)(d). The order is available on an interim basis.
why_A_wrong: A states the Violence-Prerequisite Trap. FLA s. 24(3)(f) lists violence as one factor the court must consider — it is not a precondition for an exclusive possession order. Courts grant exclusive possession in the absence of violence where other s. 24(3) factors, such as the best interests of children and availability of alternative accommodation, support the order. Requiring violence as a threshold condition misreads the FLA.
why_B_wrong: B is correct. Under FLA s. 24(1) and (3), the court weighs multiple factors in assessing exclusive possession. Here, the children's proximity to school (s. 24(3)(a) — best interests), Bernard's access to alternative accommodation (s. 24(3)(d)), and the absence of a written agreement (s. 24(3)(c)) are all relevant. No single factor is determinative, and ownership by Bernard is not a bar — s. 24(1) expressly permits the order regardless of which spouse owns the home.
why_C_wrong: C states the Ownership-Override Trap. Under FLA s. 24(1), the court may grant exclusive possession regardless of which spouse owns the home. Bernard's registered ownership is not a significant factor under s. 24(3) and is not a bar to the order. FLA s. 26 specifically preserves the titled spouse's ownership rights while allowing the other spouse to have possession — exclusive possession and ownership are separate legal concepts under the FLA.
why_D_wrong: D states the Interim-Bars-Order Trap. FLA s. 24 does not limit exclusive possession to final orders. Courts routinely grant exclusive possession on an interim motion pending the determination of the matrimonial home and parenting arrangements. The interim nature of the application does not bar the court from making the order — it affects the duration and whether conditions apply.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Exclusive Possession Orders — s. 24
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 24(1)

tested_concepts: FLA s.24(1) exclusive possession regardless of ownership

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Special Rules for the Matrimonial Home
fact_pattern: Obiageli brings a motion for exclusive possession of the matrimonial home. Both she and her husband Emeka are registered owners. The couple's three children, aged 5, 9, and 13, live in the home and attend schools within walking distance. Emeka earns $180,000 per year and can afford alternative accommodation. Obiageli earns $42,000 per year. The court must apply FLA s. 24(3) to decide the motion.
call_of_question: Which factors under FLA s. 24(3) are most directly engaged in Obiageli's favour on these facts?

options.A: The fact that Obiageli is a joint owner of the home is the determinative factor in her favour.
options.B: The best interests of the three children and the significant disparity in the parties' financial positions and ability to secure alternative accommodation are the most directly engaged s. 24(3) factors supporting Obiageli's motion.
options.C: Obiageli's motion will succeed only if she can prove that Emeka has committed family violence.
options.D: The only relevant factor is the length of the marriage; exclusive possession is routinely granted in long marriages.

explanation: Under the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 24(3), the court must consider: the best interests of the children, the financial situation of both spouses, the availability of other appropriate accommodation, any violence or abuse, and any other relevant circumstances. On these facts, the best interests of three school-age children (proximity to schools, stability) and the parties' divergent financial capacity to secure alternative accommodation (Emeka at $180,000 vs. Obiageli at $42,000) are the most directly engaged factors. Joint ownership is relevant but not determinative.
why_A_wrong: Joint ownership is a relevant fact but is not the determinative factor for an exclusive possession order. The s. 24(3) multi-factor analysis applies equally whether one or both spouses hold title.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The best interests of the children and the financial disparity between the parties are the most prominent s. 24(3) factors on these facts.
why_C_wrong: Violence is one s. 24(3) factor but is not a precondition. Exclusive possession may be ordered in the absence of violence where other factors, particularly the children's best interests, support it.
why_D_wrong: Length of marriage is not listed as a s. 24(3) factor, and exclusive possession is not automatically granted in long marriages. The statute specifies specific factors that the court must weigh.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Exclusive Possession Orders — s. 24
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 24(3)

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Special Rules for the Matrimonial Home
fact_pattern: The court grants Théodore's wife Monique an exclusive possession order over the matrimonial home for 18 months, during which Théodore is prohibited from entering or occupying the property. Théodore's lawyer later tells him that the exclusive possession order has transferred legal title to the home to Monique and that he is no longer an owner. Théodore is alarmed by this advice.
call_of_question: What is the legal effect of an exclusive possession order under FLA s. 24(1) on the ownership of the matrimonial home?

options.A: An exclusive possession order transfers legal title to the matrimonial home to the spouse who receives the order.
options.B: An exclusive possession order is a temporary right of occupation only; it does not transfer ownership or title to the home; Théodore remains a legal owner despite the order.
options.C: An exclusive possession order operates as a constructive trust, entitling Monique to a beneficial interest in the home equal to the period of exclusive occupation.
options.D: An exclusive possession order automatically converts the matrimonial home into jointly held property upon its expiry.

explanation: Under the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 24(1), an exclusive possession order grants the right to reside in and occupy the matrimonial home to one spouse for a specified period. It is a personal right of occupation, not a transfer of ownership. Legal title remains unchanged; Théodore remains a registered owner throughout the order. The order bars him from entering or occupying the home during the specified period but does not divest him of his ownership interest.
why_A_wrong: An exclusive possession order does not transfer legal title. The FLA regime for possession (Part II) is separate from property ownership. Title transfers require a vesting order or other disposition, not an exclusive possession order.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Exclusive possession under s. 24(1) is a temporary occupation right only; ownership and title are unaffected.
why_C_wrong: An exclusive possession order does not create a constructive trust or beneficial interest. Constructive trusts arise from different equitable principles (unjust enrichment, common intention). s. 24(1) is a statutory occupation right, not an equitable remedy.
why_D_wrong: The expiry of an exclusive possession order has no automatic effect on property ownership. The home reverts to its pre-order occupancy status; title remains wherever it was registered.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Exclusive Possession Orders — s. 24
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 24(1)

tested_concepts: exclusive possession is not a title transfer

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Special Rules for the Matrimonial Home
fact_pattern: Anita and Darnell separate. There have been no incidents of family violence. Anita's lawyer tells her that she cannot obtain an exclusive possession order because there has been no violence and the children, aged 16 and 17, are almost adults. Darnell's lawyer tells him that an exclusive possession order will certainly be granted because Anita is the lower-income spouse. Both lawyers assert their positions as categorical rules.
call_of_question: Which statement most accurately reflects the law on exclusive possession under FLA s. 24?

options.A: Anita's lawyer is correct: an exclusive possession order cannot be made without evidence of family violence.
options.B: Darnell's lawyer is correct: lower income alone entitles a spouse to exclusive possession.
options.C: Neither lawyer is correct; under FLA s. 24(3) the court considers multiple factors including but not limited to violence, financial position, availability of other accommodation, and best interests of children; no single factor is conclusive, and the absence of violence does not bar an order, nor does income disparity alone guarantee one.
options.D: Exclusive possession cannot be ordered where the children are teenagers because their best interests are less weighty than those of younger children.

explanation: Under the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 24(3), the court must weigh all relevant circumstances, including the best interests of any children regardless of age, the financial situation of both spouses, the availability of other appropriate accommodation, any violence or abuse, and other relevant factors. No single factor is determinative. The absence of violence does not categorically bar an exclusive possession order — other factors may support it. Equally, financial disparity alone does not guarantee an order; the court exercises discretion based on the full picture.
why_A_wrong: Violence is one s. 24(3) factor, not a precondition. The court may grant exclusive possession even where there is no violence if other factors, including the best interests of the children, the financial situation of the parties, and availability of accommodation, support the order.
why_B_wrong: Income disparity is one factor in the s. 24(3) analysis. It does not alone entitle a spouse to exclusive possession. The court weighs all factors and exercises discretion.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s. 24(3) is a multi-factor balancing test; no single element is conclusive, and both categorical positions asserted by the lawyers are incorrect.
why_D_wrong: The best interests of children are relevant under s. 24(3) regardless of the children's ages. The statute does not discount the interests of teenage children, and older children's need for stability, schooling, and community ties remains a legitimate consideration.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.heading: Exclusive Possession Orders — s. 24
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 24(3)
